telltalePatterns: /θ/ and /ð/ realized as dental stops [t̪] and [d̪]
/ɹ/ produced as an alveolar tap or trill [ɾ/r]

phonemeProblems.produced: Voiced dental stop /d̪/, as in 'deather' instead of 'weather'
Voiceless dental stop /t̪/, as in 'true' instead of 'through'
Tensed to /i/, as in 'leetle' instead of 'little'

prosody.fluencyNotes: Fluent with consistent pacing, but limited use of connected speech (linking, elision). Words are often produced as distinct units.

strengths: Good command of English grammar and vocabulary
Consistent and deliberate speech rate aids comprehension
Clear articulation of most consonant clusters (e.g., 'spring', 'stopped')

recommendedFocus.reason: Substituting these high-frequency sounds with stops is the most significant contributor to the speaker's accent and can cause lexical confusion (e.g., three/tree, then/den).
The use of a tapped/trilled 'r' is a highly salient feature of the L1 accent. Mastering the English approximant would dramatically increase the native-like quality of the speech.

recommendedFocus.area: Vowel distinction /ɪ/ vs /i/ (e.g., ship vs. sheep)
recommendedFocus.reason: This common vowel merger can lead to frequent misunderstandings of common words, impacting overall intelligibility.

result.transcript: Yesterday the weather was really beautiful, so I decided to go for a walk through the park near my house. The trees were green and the birds were singing everywhere. I think spring is my favorite time of the year. I walked along the river and watched some children playing with a big red ball. They were laughing and having so much fun together. One little girl threw the ball very high, and it landed right in the water. Her father had to reach in and pull it out. After that, I stopped at a small village shop to buy some bread and a bottle of juice.
